findings.message: `extract_type_names` is too permissive: it treats every PascalCase token in the rendered return string as a target, so signatures like `impl Display`/`dyn Debug` and wrapper-heavy returns like `Result<Option<User>, DomainError>` can produce false edges to `Display`, `Debug`, `Result`, or `Option` whenever those names exist in the graph. That makes the diagram report dependencies that do not exist; reuse the structured return-type extraction logic instead of token-splitting the string.

`extract_type_names` is still too coarse for common Rust signatures: it will surface associated-type labels like `Item` and `Output` from returns such as `impl Iterator<Item = Foo>` / `impl Future<Output = Foo>`, and the new whitelist still misses other standard wrappers/traits like `IntoIterator`, `Clone`, `Copy`, and `Default`. If any of those names exist in the `TypeGraph`, the renderer will emit false edges.

The new assoc-type label filter is too broad: once a label name like `Item`/`Output` is collected, every matching token is dropped from the return string. That silently removes valid edges when a real type shares the same name, e.g. `impl Iterator<Item = Item>` or `impl Future<Output = Output>`.

`STDLIB_WRAPPER_TYPES` still omits common standard-library trait tokens such as `TryFrom` and `TryInto`. `extract_type_names()` will emit those names for signatures like `impl TryFrom<Foo>`, so if the graph ever contains a same-named node the renderer will still create a false edge, leaving this class of dependency-noise unresolved.

`STDLIB_WRAPPER_TYPES` is still incomplete: common std wrappers such as `PhantomData` (and `Cow`) are not filtered, so a method returning `PhantomData<Foo>` can still produce a false edge whenever a `PhantomData` node exists in the graph. That defeats the new guard against wrapper-like return tokens becoming diagram edges.

`STDLIB_WRAPPER_TYPES` lists only the bare `NonZero` token here, but `extract_type_names()` emits concrete identifiers like `NonZeroU32` and `NonZeroI64` as single tokens. Those common stdlib wrappers will still pass the filter and can create false edges whenever a graph contains a same-named type.

The stdlib-wrapper denylist is still incomplete for common single-token wrappers. Types like `Poll`, `ControlFlow`, and `Range*` are not filtered here, so a method returning `Poll<Published>` or `Range<User>` will still emit false edges whenever the graph contains those node names. That leaves the same false-positive bug this patch is meant to remove.

`extract_type_names` tokenizes return types only by separators, so common associated-type bindings like `impl Iterator<Item = Foo>` or `impl Future<Output = Bar>` will surface `Item`/`Output` as candidate types. If the workspace has a type with one of those names, the renderer adds a bogus edge; this needs label-aware parsing or reuse of the structured type-name extraction instead of raw token splitting.

`render_type_graph_flat` documents `EdgeSet::Fields`, `EdgeSet::Impls`, and `EdgeSet::All` as supported modes, but the implementation only ever collects method edges. A caller that selects `Fields` or `Impls` gets an empty diagram, and `All` still omits those edge classes entirely, so the public API cannot satisfy its own contract.

The `max_nodes_per_diagram` truncation slices the alphabetically sorted connected-node set before filtering edges, so a small limit can drop every rendered edge and leave a diagram of isolated nodes even when the graph has relationships. For example, with edges `A -> Z` and `B -> C`, a limit of 2 keeps `A` and `B` but renders no edges at all. The truncation step needs to preserve an actual edge-induced subgraph, not an arbitrary node prefix.

D6 hard-codes the Phase 2 auto-render default to `--cluster-depth 2`, but the same ADR later says the Phase 2 default is still an open question between `1` and `2`. That internal contradiction leaves the implementation target ambiguous.

`spec.json` is marking inferred requirements as blue-sourced facts. The TDD item cites `.claude/rules/05-testing.md §Core Principles`, but that source only requires TDD in general and does not justify the specific `empty graph / single edge / multi-edge / enum shape` test matrix; likewise the CI acceptance text cites `§Pre-commit Checklist` for a narrower `cargo make ci` breakdown it does not define. Under `knowledge/conventions/source-attribution.md`, these items should use inference/discussion sources or cite a document that actually states them.

T002 does not include the required wiring in `apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs`, which is where `TrackCommand` and `execute()` dispatch are defined today. Following this scope literally would only add `graph.rs` and `tddd/mod.rs`, leaving `sotp track type-graph` unreachable and failing the Phase 1 acceptance criterion.

The T003 acceptance criterion still describes `cargo make ci` as only `fmt-check + clippy -D warnings + nextest + deny + check-layers`, but the current Makefile defines `ci` as the full `ci-local` suite and even `ci-rust` also runs `verify-canonical-modules-local`. This under-specifies the real gate, so the plan can mark verification complete while the actual CI command still fails.

T002 still describes CLI dispatch as a single ambiguous `mod.rs` registration here, while `spec.json` now correctly requires wiring both `apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/mod.rs` and the top-level `apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs`. Because `plan.md` is generated from this metadata, an implementer can still miss the `TrackCommand`/`execute()` wiring and end up with an unreachable `sotp track type-graph` command.

The plan/spec still describe trait-impl edges as deferred because of a future `tddd-05` dependency, but `TypeNode::trait_impls` is already present in `libs/domain/src/schema.rs`. That makes the out-of-scope note factually wrong and can mislead Phase 2 planning by treating an already-satisfied prerequisite as a blocker.
